draft.introduction: Revelation 1:1–20 opens the Apocalypse with John’s vision of the risen Christ on Patmos, whose appearance—eyes like flame, voice like many waters—draws on Daniel 7 and Ezekiel 1. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave him to show to his servants the things that must soon take place. He made it known by sending his angel to his servant John, who bore witness to the word of God and to the testimony of Jesus Christ. Blessed is the one who reads aloud the words of this prophecy, and blessed are those who hear, and who keep what is written in it, for the time is near.
translation.original_text: αποκαλυψις ιησου χριστου ην εδωκεν αυτω ο θεος

translation.text: Then I turned to see the voice that was speaking to me, and on turning I saw seven golden lampstands, and in the midst of the lampstands one like a son of man, clothed with a long robe and with a golden sash around his chest. The hairs of his head were white, like white wool, like snow. His eyes were like a flame of fire. When I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead. But he laid his right hand on me, saying, “Fear not, I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore.”
translation.original_text: και επεστρεψα βλεπειν την φωνην ητις ελαλει μετ εμου

translator_notes.note: The Greek apokalypsis (αποκάλυψις) means “unveiling” or “disclosure,” not “destruction.” The book claims to reveal hidden realities about the present cosmic order, not merely predict future catastrophe.
